## **System overview:**

Our system will be a store inventory management app. The user will be able to interact with the menus to see information about their store inventory such as logistics involving what items are selling the most and least, as well as a store inventory to keep track of their items, and an alerts page.  
![][image1]

## **Rationale for the project:**

Our rationale for creating this project is to give store owners a highly customizable way to manage their store. There are many variables in managing a store, and this software plans to give the storeowner an easy way to keep track of them, as well as giving them the ability to define how many that they need.  This provides a specialized application for store management.

Note \- someone look up similar projects to add something there 

## **Features**:

(will suggestion: database is common requirement)  
Feature 1: An alert system for when someone is running low on products

- Before: A store owner has difficulty keeping track of when they need to get new products.  
- After: They will be able to set alerts unique to a product so they know when they will run out  
  Requirements(will): A counter that keeps track of the amount of every product and also somewhere to display the alerts when it reaches past a certain threshold.  
- The target user will be those in charge of restocking for the company, and will involve the stock system for the item database sending a message based on low value. The user Value is to easily respond to lack of stock.

Brainstorming: In existing products, such as square space, it is up to owners to keep track of their own inventory. We thought this feature could help automate that process in a way where owners can set their own preference for what would constitute low-stock and notify them when that threshold is reached on a per-item basis.

Feature 2: The ability to remove a product from the database and stop tracking.

- Before: May have multiple products left within the database that are no longer sold by the store owner. Leading to convolution within the app, with multiple ghost products.  
- After: Can clean their store’s database of items that are no longer carried/stocked which can save users such as stocking staff from unnecessary confusion.  
    
  The target user will be the manager of the store database, and will involve updating the store database to no longer include an item. 

Brainstorming: This kind of behavior can happen quite frequently in the case of limited time product runs or simply in the situation where a product becomes outdated in the current market. So, in the event that a store owner wishes to delist an item from their store and subsequently from their inventory system. We thought having the ability to remove an item from the database to be important to keep a store’s database from becoming cluttered with unlisted items. 

Feature 3: The ability to add a product to the database

- Before: store owners must manually track new products on paper or in separate spreadsheets, which increases the chance of forgetting item details, mispricing, or inconsistent naming.  
- After: the system allows users to register new products directly into the inventory database, ensuring consistent formatting, accurate tracking, and immediate availability for stock updates and alerts.  
    
  The target user will be the database manager, with the user removing an item from the database system.

Brainstorming: Somewhat goes without saying that a business needs the ability to add new product types. Without this there would be no products for the database to increment and decrement as outlined in our next two feature summaries. So, this feature is core to having a working minimum viable product.

Feature 4: Incrementing the stock number of a certain item that already exists in the database

- Before: when new shipments arrive, store owners must update stock counts manually, often leading to miscounts or outdated inventory levels that affect logistics and alerts.  
- After: users can quickly increase the stock quantity of an existing product, ensuring the inventory reflects real time availability and improving accuracy for sales and restocking decisions.

        
The target user will be the store stocker, who will access the database system to increase the amount value of an existing item when stocking. This requires that the item already exists within the database.

Brainstorming: Aside from listing products it is important to be able to track the total count of each product currently on hand. Having the ability to increment the product as a standalone action also allows for easy stock updates after a potential delivery without knowing the exact total of the invoice. As well as giving another way for owners to cross check the amount of inventory actually received vs. their invoice count.

Feature 5: Decrementing the stock number of a certain item that already exists in the database

- Before: as items sell or expire, stock levels may not be updated consistently, causing the system to show incorrect quantities and preventing accurate low stock alerts.  
- After: users can decrease the stock quantity of a product as items are sold or removed, keeping the inventory accurate and enabling the alert system to function reliably.  
  The  target user will be the store manager, who will access the database to decrease the amount for items when they are making sales to the teacher.

Brainstorming: With existing products, such as square space, to decrement an item from the database one must “check-out” the item even if there is no monetary transaction. Which can lead to multiple users needing to use the register when technically speaking there is no reason for it. For example, in the case of a damaged product that needs to be removed from the database or if a product is given free of charge.  
   

## **Priority Feature: Make a Starting Menu**

Will’s Before: The user is unable to have an interface through which they can go to the options provided by the software. They would have to deal with the entirety of the interface being on a single page, which is unwieldy to interact with.  
Will’s After: The user is able to use the starting page to go to any of the other pages for the services, enabling them to choose which of the features to interact with.

Iqra’s Before: The user (a store employee), had to scroll through a cluttered interface with all features crammed onto one page. It was hard for them to find the inventory section quickly, and often clicked the wrong buttons when trying to update stock.  
Iqra’s After: With the new starting menu, the user can immediately choose whether they want to view logistics, update inventory, or check alerts. It saves time and reduces mistakes, especially when the user is in a rush during busy hours.

Phil’s Before: The user would be overwhelmed with all the different buttons, features, etc which would also cost the employee more time trying to understand or find the correct button they are searching for and they might even just go back to manually keeping track of supplies rather than deal with a disorganized mess of buttons.  
Phil’s After: The user won’t be overwhelmed because buttons with a similar or connected function will be sorted into certain menu options/buttons to quickly narrow down what they need to do and execute it.

Kenzie’s Before: Without a landing page the user may be overwhelmed with information that should otherwise be obfuscated unless searched for. New users would also not know where they need to go first for let’s say account set-up and established users would be exposed to unnecessary information that only pertains to new users.  
Kenzie’s After: With a landing page information can be properly obfuscated keeping users in a simple environment that presents the information they need in the moment. Which keeps UX and usability at the forefront of the design while retaining the ability to access less-frequently needed UI elements in a more readable and collapsable design.

**Inspiration/How we came up with the requirements:**  
Our requirements came directly from observing the everyday challenges that small store owners face when managing inventory. We noticed that many people still rely on manual tracking, scattered spreadsheets, or outdated systems, which makes it easy to lose track of stock levels, forget to reorder items, or accidentally keep discontinued products in their records. From these pain points, we identified the core actions a store owner needs to perform: adding new items, removing old items, adjusting stock levels, and receiving alerts before items run out. Each requirement was shaped around simplifying these tasks and creating a system that feels intuitive, organized, and supportive for someone running a busy store.

## **Use cases:**

* Add New Item  
* Remove item  
* Increase Item Stock  
* Decrease Item stock  
* Set Low Stock Alert

## **UML Diagram:**

![][image2]

In order to increase stock, need to include item selection  
\-Maybe customer user selects item

remove item=buy item- customer selects, then buys  
store manager replenishes 

**Note:** the “Set Low Stock Alert” use case is connected to “Decrease Stock” using an \<\<extend\>\> relationship. This means the alert feature is conditionally triggered when stock levels fall below a threshold, it’s an optional behavior that extends the main goal of decreasing stock. Also the diagram was made in Canva (I didn’t wanna draw it by hand).

## **Potential “Actors”:**

* Store Owner/Employee (User): interacts with the system to manage inventory  
* The Store Database: external system storing product data  
* Product supplier/resupplier company: external system/person that receives alerts or restock info

## **Methodology Used:**

* To gather requirements for our store inventory management app, our team began by brainstorming common pain points faced by small store owners. We will be supplementing this with informal interviews and observations of how local businesses track inventory, often using spreadsheets, paper logs, or outdated systems. We also will review similar apps and tools online to identify any gaps and opportunities for improvement.  
* We validated our requirements by checking for feasibility (can we build it?), relevance (does it solve a real problem?), and clarity (can users understand and use it easily?). Each feature was evaluated based on how directly it addressed a user need and whether it could be implemented without our project timeline/deadline.  
* We chose to follow Agile methodologies, specifically Feature Driven Development (FDD), Extreme Programming (XP), and SCRUM, because they emphasize user-centered design and iterative progress. Agile’s four core values guided our approach which are:   
  * Individuals and interactions over processes and tools: we prioritized team collaboration and user feedback  
    * We prioritized team communication, brainstorming sessions, and shared decision making. Instead of relying on rigid procedures, we focused on understanding each other’s perspectives and the real needs of store owners. This helped us refine our features in a way that felt natural and user centered   
  * Working software over comprehensive documentation: we focused on building usable features early  
    * Our goal was to build functional pieces of the system early, even simple prototypes, so we could validate whether our ideas actually solved the problems we identified. This mindset is why we focused on the starting menu as our MVP. It’s a tangible, functional entry point that lets users navigate the system immediately  
  * Customer collaboration over contract negotiation: we shaped features around real user stories  
    * We shaped our requirements around real user stories based on the challenges small store owners face. Instead of assuming what users wanted, we grounded each feature in a before and after scenario. This helped us refine vague ideas into concrete, testable requirements  
  * Responding to change over following a plan: we remained flexible and adjusted our backlog as needed  
    * As we developed user stories, we realized some assumptions needed to be adjusted. Agile allows us to revise our backlog, reorder priorities, and reshape features without feeling locked into an early plan. This flexibility is essential for making sure our requirements stay aligned with user needs  
  * These values naturally led us to choose FDD, XP, and SCRUM   
    * FDD helped focus on features as core units of value  
    * Each feature is the backlog directly addresses a user need, like adding items, removing items, or receiving low stock alerts  
    * XP supported our emphasis on user stories and iterative refinement.  
    * By writing before and after scenarios, we clarified the purpose of each feature and make sure it delivered real value  
    * SCRUM gave a structured way to manage our work through sprints  
    * Breaking the MVP into tasks and assigning them to our team members allowed us to stay organized and make good consistent progress  
* User stories helped us refine features by grounding them in real world scenarios. SCRUM lets us manage our work in sprints, making sure there’s steady progress and accountability.

**Similar Products:**  
	Each of these inventory tracking websites on the market have a few features which are prevalent among them all. The ability to track real time stock of what goes in and out of inventory, multiple storage location management, low stock alerts, look up and input by barcode or qr code, and an audit log of all changes done to the inventory from the website. These websites give us a basis for what is expected with an inventory tracker and what we can improve on. 

* With large scale inventory managers like Odoo, navigating their complex interface can be hard on a small business owner who is already juggling many other jobs. Our product aims to provide a simple UI for ease of use, while still providing key analytics.  
* Supplier management is absent in other inventory managers; providing a way of tracking what suppliers are involved with a store owners products would be helpful for keeping aspects of inventory in one place

**Rationality Behind Requirements:**

* Each feature must have a reason for why it should be considered and implemented into the product. These reasons can come from the perspective of what the user wants and requires of the product or what our team could be limited in when creating and implementing the feature.   
* Our main factors for considering the requirements is if it is even feasible to do with the team’s resources, if the description of the asked for feature is detailed enough for our team to accurately make a model of, if the feature is necessary or redundant, and lastly, if the feature can be consistently used by the user without the presence of encountering bugs constantly.  
* By listening to our user’s stories about what they need in a store inventory management app, we were able to create and connect many features and functionalities to these user stories like when the user often ran out a stock without knowing, the idea for an alert feature to notify the user when stock of an item was low, when the user asked how they would exit a UI, the plan for the basic functionality of a close/return button in order to return to the home screen was added to our team’s list of functionalities.  
* Following an agile development plan, our team will continue to listen to user stories in order to communicate efficiently with the client and also provide them with a cohesive and satisfying product for them to use because if our team doesn’t take into account our user’s stories when implementing and creating features and functionalities, the final product might not align with what the user is looking for and/or is missing key features and functionalities that could improve the design and usability of our product to the client which would leave the client and our development team both with a feeling of dissatisfaction towards the product.

**TEAM SPLIT FOR METHODOLOGY SECTION:**  
✅Iqra: write the part about agile values and why you chose FDD, XP, and SCRUM (explain 4 agile values and connect agile to how our team refined features and user stories)  
Kenzie: write the part about how you validated requirements (feasibility, relevance, clarity, technical constraints, user centered evaluation)  
Will: write the part about how you gathered requirements (brainstorming, observing store workflows, researching similar apps, and identifying pain points)  
✅Keshav: add examples of similar apps you looked at and what they lacked and how our system can improve on that  
✅Phil: write about how the team evaluated requirements using criteria like validity, feasibility, clarity, consistency, and testability. Also explain how the team maintained requirement traceability, linking features to user needs, user stories to features, and functionalities to user stories and why it’s important in agile development. 

## **MVP Project Management Plan:**

* Our MVP focuses on the highest priority feature: the starting menu. This feature allows users to navigate between inventory, logistics, and alert pages, solving the problem of a cluttered single page interface.  
* We broke this feature into the following developer tasks:  
  * Design the starting menu layout  
  * Implement navigation buttons for each page  
  * Connect buttons to backend routes  
  * Test menu functionality across devices  
  * Refine UI for accessibility and clarity  
* We organized these tasks into a two week sprint using SCRUM. Each team member was assigned tasks  
  * Iqra: UI design and accessibility testing  
    * We chose the starting menu as our MVP because it’s the core entry point for the entire system. Without it, users would be forced to navigate a cluttered single page interface, making it difficult to access inventory, logistics, or alerts efficiently. The starting menu immediately improves usability by organizing all major features into clear, simple options. It also establishes the technical foundation for the rest of the app by setting up navigation routes and page structure. Because it delivers instant user value and supports every future feature, it’s the most essential piece to build first.  
  * Kenzie: documentation and GitHub issue tracking  
  * Will: frontend implementation and layout  
  * Keshav: testing and bug fixes  
    * Everything testing and bug related will be tracked on a spreadsheet categorized by each feature of the website as follows.   
      * Pages is the first grouping, each page would get its own section  
        * Features \- under the page section would be the features that page contains  
          * Front end test \- covering tests on what the user sees and interactions. Looking into if buttons show up, properly providing error messages to the user, does it respond as intended  
          * Backend test \- coverage what happens what a button is clicked on, looking if the database is updated, how does it handle bad input, does it return proper responses to front end  
        * Each aspect of the button and how it was tested will also be considered for tracking such as:  
          * intended purpose \- a description of what the feature is supposed to do  
          * how the feature was tested \- manually or through code, and provides details on how it was tested for both ways  
          * actual outcome \- if the test matches expected or fails to match, if test went wrong include what was wrong about it   
          * Code location \- a reference to where the feature was implemented in the code  
  * Phil: backend routing and integration  
* We prioritized this feature because it directly improves usability and set the foundation for all other features. User stories guided our breakdown, each task starts and ends with the user's experience. Our effort estimates included time for testing and revision to make it a smooth experience.

## **Description of Frontend Tasks** 

* Our menu’s UI will have a large rectangle with the word “Home” on the top middle of the UI in order to let the user know that they are currently in the menu.  
* Below the “Home” box there will be 3 different buttons centered around the middle of the whole UI in the form of 3 spaced rectangular boxes, each with the words “Inventory”, “Logistics”, and “Alert” for each button respectively which will all lead to the “Inventory”, “Logistics”, and “Alert” pages respectively.  
* The background of the Home page will most likely be a darker color while the Home, Inventory, Logistics, and Alerts will all be the same contrasting color to the Home page background in order to better catch the attention of the user.

**Description of Backend Task and Relation to Frontend Tasks**: 

* Clicking on the “Inventory” button in the menu will show a different UI to the user with the main purpose of this UI being to see the store’s inventory and also order more stock for that item if the item’s stock is running low.  
  * There will be a list of different “Categories” will have its own sublist of the item names to help keep the list of the various stock more organized for more ease of user for the user when quickly wanting to find a specific item  
    * Each item will give a short description which selected which will originally be set by the user in order to inform other users what the item is/does so they can also describe what it does to the store’s customers if they ask questions about the product(s)  
    * When an item has been selected/clicked on by the user it will pop up with a price of how much it cost for the store to purchase new stock from a supplier  
    * When clicking on an item, it will also pop up a add item and remove item button to give the user the ability to choose how much of that item they want order/remove from the order before it is send to the supplier  
  * There will be a button to add and remove items as a whole from categories if the store is selling new stuff or discontinuing to sell an item in the store  
  * There will be a button to go back to the main menu in the top corner of the “Inventory” page to allow users to quickly go back to the menu  
* Clicking on the “Logistics” button in the menu will show a different UI to the user with the main purpose of this UI being to see the quantity of items sold recently to see what is popular and also to see what items have not been sold/selling well recently.  
  * There will be two separate lists in the “Logistics” page with the top list showing the 3 most bought items recently and the bottom list showing items that haven’t been sold much recently while probably also being scrollable depending on if there is a lot of items that end up on that list  
  * Top selling list will go from 1st most selling(top) to 3rd most selling(bottom) with a quantity amount next to each one  
  * Lower demand item list will count when items were last sold and put at least the 3 items that have gone the longest without anyone buying any. Also each of the items will state how long it has been since anyone bought that item  
  * There will be a button to go back to the main menu in the top corner of the “Logistics” page to allow users to quickly go back to the menu  
* Clicking on the “Alerts” button in the menu will show a different UI to the user with the main purpose of this UI being to provide a notification to the user(s) when an item’s stock goes under a certain threshold.  
  * If there is a notification/alert for the user, the “Alerts” button in the menu will change its appearance with the addition of a red circle in the corner of the button with a white/black exclamation mark inside of the circle to standout and get the user’s attention  
  * Within the “Alerts” page, there will be rectangles that will pop up a smaller UI that will contain the notification of which item is currently low in stock of and will also come along with an “X” in the corner of the notification to let users close the notification whenever they want/are finished reading it  
    * Notifications will be in the order of oldest(bottom) to newest(top)  
  * If a notification has not been read, it will have a brightly colored exclamation mark to let the user know that they have not read that notification yet and it will disappear permanently when the has user clicked on the notification once  
  * There will be a button to go back to the main menu in the top corner of the “Alerts” page to allow users to quickly go back to the menu  
* 

**TEAM SPLIT FOR MVP SECTION:**

Kenzie: write the GitHub setup and how issues/sprints will be managed
